title: Anatomy of a Data Breach
intro: It's 2 AM and your phone is blowing up. A user just tweeted a screenshot showing they can see other users' private data on your app. Just like a burst pipe, your first job is to stop the leak before doing anything else. This is a real security incident, and every decision you make in the next few minutes matters.

teach.title: A Data Breach Is Like a Burst Pipe
teach.body: When a pipe bursts in your house, the first thing you do is shut off the water -- not mop the floor, not call the plumber, not post on social media. In a data breach, your first move is the same: stop the leak before doing anything else.

teach.title: Incident Response Is Like an ER Triage
teach.body: When someone arrives at the emergency room, doctors don't start with paperwork -- they stabilize the patient first, then diagnose, then treat. A data breach works the same way: contain the bleeding (stop the leak), assess the damage (check logs), notify the family (tell affected users), and then perform surgery (fix the root cause). Skipping straight to the fix while data is still leaking is like scheduling surgery while the patient is still in the ambulance.

teach.title: The Four Steps of Incident Response
teach.body:
When a security breach happens, follow these steps in order:

1. **Contain** -- stop the leak immediately (disable the endpoint, revoke the key)
2. **Assess** -- check logs to see how much data was accessed
3. **Notify** -- tell affected users and your team
4. **Fix** -- patch the vulnerability and add safeguards to prevent it from happening again

teach.title: A Common API Vulnerability
teach.annotation: The vulnerable version lets anyone fetch any user's data just by changing the ID in the URL. The fix adds authentication and checks that you can only access your own data.

teach.body: If you're building apps with AI and selling them to clients, a single data breach can destroy the trust you worked so hard to build -- and kill your revenue overnight. Knowing how to respond to incidents isn't just a skill, it's insurance for your business.
In a breach, contain first, then assess scope, notify users, and fix the root cause. Every API endpoint needs authorization checks -- never assume the frontend will prevent unauthorized access.

start.narrative: You see the tweet: a user navigated to /api/users/42 and got back another user's full profile including email, phone number, and hashed password. Your API is leaking private data. What's your first move?

choices.text: Immediately restrict the vulnerable API endpoint
Reply to the tweet thanking them for the report
Check if any other endpoints have the same problem

restrict-endpoint.narrative: Good -- stop the bleeding first. You add authentication checks to the endpoint so only authenticated users can access their own data. The immediate leak is plugged. What's your next step?

choices.text: Check your server logs to see how many users' data was accessed
Go back to sleep -- the fix is deployed
Audit all other API endpoints for the same vulnerability

reply-tweet.narrative: Acknowledging the report publicly is fine, but the endpoint is still leaking data while you compose tweets. Every minute it stays open, more user data could be scraped. Containment first, communication second.

choices.text: Right -- let me restrict the endpoint first

check-others-first.narrative: Checking scope is smart, but the reported endpoint is actively leaking data right now. An attacker could be scraping every user's profile while you're auditing. Plug the known leak first, then investigate the scope.

choices.text: Restrict the reported endpoint first, then check others

check-logs.narrative: Your logs show 347 requests to /api/users/{id} with sequential IDs from a single IP address in the past 6 hours. Someone was systematically scraping user profiles. This means the data of up to 347 users may be compromised. What do you do?

choices.text: Notify affected users and your team, then audit all endpoints
Block the attacker's IP and call it done

audit-endpoints.narrative: You find 3 more endpoints with the same problem -- no authorization checks. You fix them all. But you haven't yet determined how much data was already accessed.

choices.text: Check the logs to determine the scope of the breach

ignore-scope.narrative: The endpoint is fixed, but you have no idea how much data was already leaked, whether other endpoints have the same issue, or whether affected users need to be notified. In many jurisdictions, failing to report a data breach is illegal. This isn't over.

choices.text: You're right -- let me investigate the full scope

end-good.narrative: You contained the leak, determined the scope, notified affected users, audited all endpoints, and added authorization checks across the board. You also added rate limiting to prevent future scraping attempts. Your team sets up automated security testing to catch this class of bug before it ships.
end-good.feedback: Textbook incident response: contain, assess scope, notify, remediate, and improve. The key insight is that authorization (who can access what) must be checked on every API endpoint, not just the frontend. This vulnerability is called Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA) and it's the #1 item on the OWASP API Security Top 10.

end-mediocre.narrative: Blocking one IP is like locking one door while leaving the windows open. The attacker can use a different IP, VPN, or proxy. You haven't notified affected users, haven't checked for other vulnerable endpoints, and may be violating data breach notification laws.
end-mediocre.feedback: IP blocking is not a security fix -- it's a speed bump. The real fix was the authorization check you added to the endpoint. But incident response doesn't end at containment: you need to assess the full scope, notify affected users, audit for similar issues, and put safeguards in place to prevent recurrence.
